issues.title: backend: add a first Smallville location expansion pack using approved proposals

labels.name: help wanted
labels.description: Extra attention is needed

labels.name: community
labels.description: Open to community contributors and visible on the Townhall board

labels.name: backend
labels.description: World API, seed data, database, or backend simulation work

issues.body:
## Summary
Implement a small, coherent location expansion pack for Smallville using approved content proposals.

## Foundation
This issue should build on the location guidance in #1 and ideally use content proposals from #17 and/or #21.

## Scope
- [ ] Add a small set of new locations
- [ ] Add matching adjacency/travel-time data
- [ ] Keep the expansion coherent and easy to review
- [ ] Verify nearby-location behavior and pathfinding still make sense after the change
- [ ] Note which source proposals were implemented

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] The new locations are internally consistent and connected properly
- [ ] Pathfinding and nearby-location behavior still work as expected
- [ ] The additions do not require a schema redesign

## Recommended order
1. #1 contributor guide for locations/adjacency
2. #17 location proposals and/or #21 venue concepts
3. This issue (#23)

## Notes
This issue is for additive seed data, not changes to the location model itself.

issues.title: frontend: add movement interpolation for agent markers on location changes

labels.name: help wanted
labels.description: Extra attention is needed

labels.name: community
labels.description: Open to community contributors and visible on the Townhall board

labels.name: frontend
labels.description: Next.js, Phaser, panels, or visualization work

issues.body:
## Summary
Smooth out agent movement on the frontend so marker updates feel more alive when location changes arrive.

## Scope
- [ ] Build a lightweight interpolation strategy for marker movement between known location anchors
- [ ] Trigger interpolation from the current frontend event/snapshot flow
- [ ] Keep the implementation small and robust for the placeholder engine stage
- [ ] Document any assumptions or limitations clearly

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Marker updates are smoother than the current snap-to-location behavior
- [ ] The implementation does not break if websocket events arrive quickly or out of order
- [ ] The work remains compatible with the current placeholder map layout

## Notes
Please keep this incremental. This issue is not asking for a full path animation system.

issues.title: frontend: refine the placeholder town map layout and location tile styling

labels.name: help wanted
labels.description: Extra attention is needed

labels.name: community
labels.description: Open to community contributors and visible on the Townhall board

labels.name: frontend
labels.description: Next.js, Phaser, panels, or visualization work

issues.body:
## Summary
Polish the maintainer-owned frontend engine's placeholder town map so it is easier to scan and more pleasant to use during playtests.

## Foundation
This issue builds on the new `frontend/` simulation foundation (Next.js + Phaser + websocket event feed). It is **not** asking for a new renderer architecture.

## Scope
- [ ] Improve the visual layout of location anchors/tiles on the placeholder Phaser map
- [ ] Make location labels easier to read without cluttering the map
- [ ] Improve spacing, contrast, or tile treatment so the map feels more coherent during debugging
- [ ] Keep the work compatible with the current `map_x` / `map_y` location layout

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] The placeholder map is noticeably easier to read than the first MVP version
- [ ] Location names and markers remain understandable at a glance
- [ ] The work stays additive and does not replace the frontend engine architecture

## Notes
This is a community-open polish issue. Please avoid changing the Phaser/React data flow itself.

issues.title: backend: add a second neighborhood-style location expansion with matching props and content hooks

labels.name: help wanted
labels.description: Extra attention is needed

labels.name: community
labels.description: Open to community contributors and visible on the Townhall board

labels.name: backend
labels.description: World API, seed data, database, or backend simulation work

issues.body:
## Summary
After the first expansion pack lands, add a second, slightly richer location expansion that feels like a cohesive new pocket of town.

## Foundation
This is a follow-on issue intended for after #23 and likely after some content groundwork from #17 and #21.

## Scope
- [ ] Add a coherent cluster of related locations rather than isolated one-off spots
- [ ] Add matching objects/props where needed to support the new area
- [ ] Make sure adjacency and travel times still feel sensible
- [ ] Call out what future jobs, events, or content the new pocket of town enables

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] The expansion feels like one neighborhood/pocket rather than scattered map noise
- [ ] Pathfinding and nearby relationships still make sense
- [ ] The work remains additive and reviewable

## Recommended order
1. #17 location ideas and/or #21 venue concepts
2. #23 first location expansion pack
3. This issue (#35)

## Notes
This issue is intentionally for a later wave once the first expansion proves out well.

issues.title: backend: add a venue-focused location, object, and consumable seed pack from approved concepts

labels.name: help wanted
labels.description: Extra attention is needed

labels.name: community
labels.description: Open to community contributors and visible on the Townhall board

labels.name: backend
labels.description: World API, seed data, database, or backend simulation work

issues.body:
## Summary
Take one approved venue concept and turn it into a tightly scoped implementation pack across locations, objects, and consumables.

## Foundation
This issue should build on the conceptual work in #21 and may also use outputs from #17, #20, #23, #24, or #25 where appropriate.

## Scope
- [ ] Pick one approved venue concept suitable for first implementation
- [ ] Add the location(s) needed for that venue if they do not already exist
- [ ] Add the world objects needed to make the venue believable
- [ ] Add a small consumable/item pack associated with that venue
- [ ] Keep the change coherent and reviewable rather than sprawling

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] The result feels like one clear venue implementation rather than three unrelated seed edits
- [ ] The work stays inside current schema/model boundaries
- [ ] The PR clearly states which concept it implements and what changed

## Recommended order
1. #21 venue concepts
2. Optional supporting content from #17 and #20
3. Supporting seed issues like #23/#24/#25 where needed
4. This issue (#34)

## Notes
This is a great "bring one new place to life" issue for the community.

issues.title: backend: add a notice-board demo content pack from approved prompt ideas

labels.name: help wanted
labels.description: Extra attention is needed

labels.name: backend

labels.name: content
labels.description: Lore, prompts, locations, jobs, menus, and item concepts

issues.body:
## Summary
Turn approved notice-board prompt ideas into a reusable demo/content pack for the project.

## Foundation
This issue should build on content work from #19 and stay compatible with the current board model.

## Scope
- [ ] Select a strong subset of approved notice-board prompts
- [ ] Organize them into a reusable content/demo pack (seed examples, fixtures, docs, or another clearly reviewable format)
- [ ] Keep the work grounded in the current board feature set
- [ ] Document how maintainers can use the pack during demos or testing

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] The result is concrete and reusable, not just another brainstorm list
- [ ] The content fits the current board/town tone
- [ ] The implementation does not require changing the board architecture

## Recommended order
1. #19 notice-board prompt writing
2. This issue (#33)

## Notes
This is intentionally flexible as long as the output is concrete and usable.

issues.title: content: write 25 notice board posts and prompts for later demo packs

labels.name: good first issue
labels.description: Good for newcomers

labels.name: community
labels.description: Open to community contributors and visible on the Townhall board

labels.name: content
labels.description: Lore, prompts, locations, jobs, menus, and item concepts

issues.body:
## Summary
Write a set of notice board posts/prompts that can make the city feel alive and later feed directly into a reusable demo/content pack.

## Why this matters
This is a content-foundation issue for follow-up work like #33.

## Scope
- [ ] Create 25 short notice board posts or prompts
- [ ] Include a mix of practical, social, funny, and slightly dramatic town messages
- [ ] Keep them grounded in Smallville rather than generic fantasy/RPG content
- [ ] Group them loosely if useful (events, errands, rumors, requests, etc.)
- [ ] Highlight a smaller subset that would make the strongest first demo pack

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] The set is varied and reusable
- [ ] The prompts fit the tone of the sim
- [ ] The contribution is easy to review and copy into later demo/content work

## Follow-up issues this should feed
- #33 backend: add a notice-board demo content pack from approved prompt ideas

## Notes
Small, high-quality prompt sets are extremely welcome.

issues.title: backend: add regression checks for community seed-data additions

labels.name: help wanted
labels.description: Extra attention is needed

labels.name: community
labels.description: Open to community contributors and visible on the Townhall board

labels.name: backend
labels.description: World API, seed data, database, or backend simulation work

issues.body:
## Summary
Add or document a lightweight regression check workflow for community seed-data PRs so maintainers can review them safely and contributors can validate their own work.

## Scope
- [ ] Define the minimum checks contributors should run after changing locations, objects, or consumables
- [ ] Add test or validation coverage where practical for pathfinding, location lookups, and `use_item` behavior
- [ ] Keep the workflow simple enough for community contributors to follow locally
- [ ] Document what signals a seed-data PR is safe to merge

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] A contributor changing seed data has a concrete validation checklist or test path
- [ ] The checks cover the most common regression risks for location/object/consumable additions
- [ ] The solution stays lightweight and does not require new architecture

## Recommended order
This can be worked on in parallel, but it becomes especially valuable as #22, #23, #24, and #25 start landing.

## Notes
This is a very useful issue for contributors who care about reliability more than new content.

issues.title: backend: add the first cafe-and-grocery consumable content pack

labels.name: help wanted
labels.description: Extra attention is needed

labels.name: community
labels.description: Open to community contributors and visible on the Townhall board

labels.name: backend
labels.description: World API, seed data, database, or backend simulation work

issues.body:
## Summary
Create a coherent seed-data content pack focused on realistic cafe and grocery consumables.

## Foundation
This issue should build on the contributor/item guidance in #2 and the concept work in #20. It can also reference venue ideas from #21 if useful.

## Scope
- [ ] Add a curated set of cafe-oriented consumables
- [ ] Add a curated set of grocery/basic pantry consumables
- [ ] Keep item naming and tone consistent with Smallville
- [ ] Make the pack easy to review as one coherent PR
- [ ] Note which concepts or venues inspired the additions

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] The content pack is additive and requires no schema changes
- [ ] The items fit the current `use_item` / vitals direction
- [ ] The additions are grouped and documented clearly in the PR

## Recommended order
1. #2 contributor guide for items/consumables
2. #20 item concepts
3. Optional: #21 venue concepts
4. This issue (#25)

## Notes
Think of this as a content-heavy backend contribution rather than a systems redesign.

issues.title: backend: add a first world-object expansion pack for beds, seating, and public props

labels.name: help wanted
labels.description: Extra attention is needed

labels.name: community
labels.description: Open to community contributors and visible on the Townhall board

labels.name: backend
labels.description: World API, seed data, database, or backend simulation work

issues.body:
## Summary
Expand the current world object seed data with a coherent first pass of beds, seating, and public/shop props.

## Foundation
This issue should build on approved location or venue ideas from #17 and #21 where useful, while staying inside the current world object model.

## Scope
- [ ] Add more beds where appropriate for homes or sleep spaces
- [ ] Add benches/chairs/tables/seating where it makes sense
- [ ] Add a small set of shop/public props that make the town feel more real
- [ ] Keep object naming and placement consistent with the current seed style
- [ ] Note which locations or venue concepts the new objects support

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] The new objects are additive and coherent
- [ ] The data is easy to review and understand
- [ ] The work stays within the current world object model

## Recommended order
1. #17 location proposals and/or #21 venue concepts
2. #23 if new locations are needed first
3. This issue (#24)

## Notes
This issue is especially helpful for future sleep and frontend visualization work.

issues.title: backend: add a first consumable seed pack from approved item concepts

labels.name: help wanted

labels.description: Open to community contributors and visible on the Townhall board

labels.name: backend
labels.description: World API, seed data, database, or backend simulation work

issues.body:
## Summary
Add a first batch of new consumable seed data using the current schema and placeholder vitals model.

## Foundation
This issue should build on the current schema and preferably draw from approved concepts in #20 and the contributor guidance in #2.

## Scope
- [ ] Add a sensible batch of food, water, and/or sleep consumables to seed data
- [ ] Use the existing consumable fields only
- [ ] Keep values conservative and reviewable
- [ ] Note clearly which concepts were implemented and where
- [ ] Verify the additions work with the current `use_item` flow

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] The new items fit the current schema without migrations
- [ ] Seed data remains coherent and easy to review
- [ ] The additions can be exercised with the existing CLI/API flow

## Recommended order
1. #2 contributor guide for items/consumables
2. #20 item concept design
3. This issue (#22)

## Notes
Please do not change the consumable architecture in this issue.

issues.title: content: propose three new Smallville venues that can become location and item packs

labels.name: help wanted
labels.description: Extra attention is needed

labels.name: community
labels.description: Open to community contributors and visible on the Townhall board

labels.name: content
labels.description: Lore, prompts, locations, jobs, menus, and item concepts

issues.body:
## Summary
Design three new venues the community wants in Smallville, with enough detail to feed directly into later seed-data implementation work.

## Why this matters
This is a content-foundation issue that should generate concrete follow-up work for locations, objects, and content packs.

## Scope
- [ ] Propose three venue concepts (diner, bakery, workshop, clinic, etc.)
- [ ] For each venue, include a short description, likely location fit, and menu/offerings
- [ ] Suggest which starter agents or future NPCs might interact with the venue
- [ ] Keep each concept practical for the current town size and current architecture
- [ ] Highlight which venue should be implemented first

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Each venue is specific enough to drive later implementation issues
- [ ] Menus/offerings are practical and not wildly outside current scope
- [ ] The write-up is easy to turn into location, object, and item follow-ups

## Follow-up issues this should feed
- #23 backend: add Smallville expansion seed data for new locations and adjacency edges
- #24 backend: add world object seed data for beds, seating, and shop props
- #25 backend: add a first community content pack for cafe and grocery consumables
- #34 backend: add a venue-focused location+object+consumable seed pack from approved concepts

## Notes
This is intentionally creative, but it should still be implementation-friendly.

issues.title: content: create 12 consumable item concepts that can become real seed data

labels.name: good first issue
labels.description: Good for newcomers

labels.name: community
labels.description: Open to community contributors and visible on the Townhall board

labels.name: content
labels.description: Lore, prompts, locations, jobs, menus, and item concepts

issues.body:
## Summary
Design a set of consumable items that can feed directly into later seed-data additions.

## Why this matters
This is a content-foundation issue for backend additions like #22 and #25.

## Scope
- [ ] Propose at least 12 consumable items
- [ ] For each item, include a name, short description, and intended type/effect (food, water, stamina, or sleep)
- [ ] Keep values modest and practical for the current placeholder vitals logic
- [ ] Group items into sensible categories (for example: cafe, grocery, college, park)
- [ ] Highlight which subset would make the best first implementation pack

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] The concepts are specific enough to become seed-data PRs
- [ ] The set includes a healthy mix of item types
- [ ] The proposal stays within the current placeholder vitals model

## Follow-up issues this should feed
- #22 backend: add additional consumable seed data for food, water, and sleep items
- #25 backend: add a first community content pack for cafe and grocery consumables

## Notes
This issue is about content design, not changing the item schema.

issues.title: content: propose 10 new Smallville locations that can turn into seed-data expansions

labels.name: help wanted
labels.description: Extra attention is needed

labels.name: community
labels.description: Open to community contributors and visible on the Townhall board

labels.name: content
labels.description: Lore, prompts, locations, jobs, menus, and item concepts

issues.body:
## Summary
Design 10 additional Smallville locations that can feed directly into later seed-data implementation work.

## Why this matters
This is a content-foundation issue. Strong proposals here should feed directly into implementation work like #23 and location/object follow-ups.

## Scope
- [ ] Propose 10 new locations that fit the current town tone
- [ ] Include a short description for each location
- [ ] Note what activities, jobs, or events each location could support
- [ ] Call out the 3-5 strongest candidates for immediate implementation
- [ ] Keep everything grounded in current town scale and current architecture

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] The set feels cohesive with the starter town
- [ ] The strongest candidates are obvious enough for maintainers/contributors to implement next
- [ ] The write-up is implementation-friendly, not just broad brainstorming

## Follow-up issues this should feed
- #23 backend: add Smallville expansion seed data for new locations and adjacency edges
- #24 backend: add world object seed data for beds, seating, and shop props

## Notes
This is content/worldbuilding work, not a request to change the location model itself.

issues.title: docs: write the contributor guide for adding locations and adjacency edges

labels.name: good first issue
labels.description: Good for newcomers

labels.name: community
labels.description: Open to community contributors and visible on the Townhall board

labels.name: docs
labels.description: Documentation, onboarding, and contributor guides

issues.body:
## Summary
Write the canonical contributor guide for adding new locations and adjacency edges to Smallville.

## Why this matters
This is a foundation issue for later community implementation work like #23. Contributors should have one clear place to learn how location expansion works before they start editing seed data.

## Scope
- [ ] Explain where location seed data lives today
- [ ] Explain how adjacency and travel time data are modeled
- [ ] Include one small worked example: add a location plus its edges
- [ ] Explain how contributors should validate pathfinding and nearby-location behavior after a change
- [ ] Link to any relevant scripts or validation docs

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] A contributor can read this guide and reasonably submit a PR for a new location expansion
- [ ] The guide documents the current model rather than proposing new architecture
- [ ] The guide is specific enough to be linked from Townhall and future backend issues

## Follow-up issues this should unlock
- #23 backend: add Smallville expansion seed data for new locations and adjacency edges

## Notes
Comment `/claim` if you want to own this documentation issue.

issues.title: frontend: replace placeholder agent circles with sprite-ready marker rendering

labels.name: help wanted
labels.description: Extra attention is needed

labels.name: community
labels.description: Open to community contributors and visible on the Townhall board

labels.name: frontend
labels.description: Next.js, Phaser, panels, or visualization work

labels.name: art
labels.description: Visual assets, sprites, tiles, props, and art direction

issues.body:
## Summary
Upgrade the current agent markers so the frontend is ready to display better visuals than colored circles.

## Scope
- [ ] Replace or wrap the current marker rendering with a sprite-ready presentation layer
- [ ] Keep the implementation compatible with the current Phaser map and selected-agent flow
- [ ] Support a reasonable fallback if final sprite assets are not available yet
- [ ] Keep the rendering path simple enough for later art iteration

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] The agent rendering layer is cleaner and more extensible than the current MVP circles
- [ ] The feature can work with placeholder or first-pass sprite assets
- [ ] The implementation does not require frontend architecture changes

## Notes
This issue is intentionally about rendering polish, not simulation state changes.

issues.title: frontend: integrate first-pass tile and prop art into the Phaser town map

issues.body:
## Summary
Start replacing the plain placeholder map surface with a first pass of actual visual assets.

## Scope
- [ ] Integrate tile or prop assets into the Phaser map in a minimal but real way
- [ ] Keep the implementation compatible with the current engine and placeholder location layout
- [ ] Use the first-pass art direction/assets available in the repo or linked issues
- [ ] Preserve debug readability while improving visual quality

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] The map looks more like a real town scene than a pure placeholder board
- [ ] Asset integration remains incremental and reviewable
- [ ] The work does not force a renderer or coordinate-system redesign

## Notes
This issue pairs well with the separate art-direction and asset-creation issues already on the board.

issues.title: frontend: add pan and zoom controls to the Phaser town view

labels.name: frontend
labels.description: Next.js, Phaser, panels, or visualization work

issues.body:
## Summary
Improve navigation inside the Phaser town view by adding basic camera controls.

## Scope
- [ ] Add simple pan and zoom support to the town map
- [ ] Keep controls intuitive for mouse/trackpad users
- [ ] Make sure labels/markers remain usable while zooming
- [ ] Avoid overcomplicating the initial control scheme

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Users can inspect different parts of the map more easily
- [ ] Camera controls do not interfere with future click-to-inspect behavior
- [ ] The implementation works well with the current placeholder renderer

## Notes
This is a good usability upgrade once the foundation is in place.

issues.title: frontend: add activity labels or hover affordances for agent markers

labels.name: help wanted
labels.description: Extra attention is needed

labels.name: community
labels.description: Open to community contributors and visible on the Townhall board

labels.name: frontend
labels.description: Next.js, Phaser, panels, or visualization work

issues.body:
## Summary
Improve map readability by showing lightweight agent context directly on the map.

## Scope
- [ ] Add simple activity labels, hover cards, or other small affordances for agent markers
- [ ] Keep the display uncluttered even with multiple agents in view
- [ ] Use existing snapshot data only; do not invent new backend requirements
- [ ] Make sure the presentation works with the current placeholder renderer

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Viewers can understand more about each visible agent without opening another panel every time
- [ ] The map remains readable and not overly noisy
- [ ] The feature degrades gracefully if some agent fields are missing or empty

## Notes
This is good polish work for contributors who like small but meaningful UX improvements.

issues.title: frontend: add click-to-inspect bridge between Phaser markers and React panels

labels.name: help wanted
labels.description: Extra attention is needed

labels.name: community
labels.description: Open to community contributors and visible on the Townhall board

labels.name: frontend
labels.description: Next.js, Phaser, panels, or visualization work

issues.body:
## Summary
Add the first clean interaction bridge between the Phaser map and the React UI panels by letting users select agents directly from the map.

## Scope
- [ ] Make agent markers on the Phaser map clickable
- [ ] Propagate the selected agent id into shared frontend state
- [ ] Ensure React-side panels (like the future inspector) can react to that selection cleanly
- [ ] Keep the bridge simple and maintainable

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Clicking an agent marker selects that agent in frontend state
- [ ] The selected agent can be consumed by React panels without hacks
- [ ] The work stays within the current frontend engine architecture

## Notes
This is one of the most important first follow-up issues after the frontend MVP foundation.

issues.title: frontend: build a world status and future-controls sidebar shell

labels.name: help wanted
labels.description: Extra attention is needed

labels.name: community
labels.description: Open to community contributors and visible on the Townhall board

labels.name: frontend
labels.description: Next.js, Phaser, panels, or visualization work

issues.body:
## Summary
Build a sidebar/panel that shows current world status and reserves space for future simulation controls.

## Foundation
This issue should build on the current frontend engine and current backend capabilities. It should not invent new control architecture on its own.

## Scope
- [ ] Show current world time / time of day
- [ ] Show websocket connection status and other helpful debug status if useful
- [ ] Show clearly labeled placeholders or disabled stubs for future actions like pause/resume, inject event, or add agent
- [ ] Keep the layout clean and easy to expand later

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] The sidebar is useful right now even before all future controls exist
- [ ] Placeholder actions are explicitly marked as future or disabled if they are not wired
- [ ] The implementation does not assume backend features that do not exist yet

## Notes
This is intentionally a shell issue, not a request to finalize simulation-control architecture.

issues.title: frontend: upgrade the raw websocket event feed into a filterable playtest panel

labels.name: help wanted
labels.description: Extra attention is needed

labels.name: community
labels.description: Open to community contributors and visible on the Townhall board

labels.name: frontend
labels.description: Next.js, Phaser, panels, or visualization work

issues.body:
## Summary
Turn the current raw websocket event list into a much better event feed for playtesting and debugging.

## Foundation
The maintainer-owned frontend engine already exposes a raw `/ws/events` feed. This issue improves that UI rather than inventing a new event transport.

## Scope
- [ ] Keep the feed backed by live websocket events from the World API
- [ ] Add client-side filtering for fields like event type, location, and agent target where practical
- [ ] Improve readability for long payloads or repeated events
- [ ] Make the feed more useful during playtests without losing raw debugging value

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] The feed remains live and websocket-backed
- [ ] Filters are usable and clearly presented
- [ ] The panel is easier to scan than the current raw MVP view

## Notes
This is a strong community issue because it is visible, bounded, and useful for everyone testing the sim.

issues.title: frontend: build an agent inspector drawer wired to selected agent snapshot data

labels.name: help wanted
labels.description: Extra attention is needed

labels.name: community
labels.description: Open to community contributors and visible on the Townhall board

labels.name: frontend
labels.description: Next.js, Phaser, panels, or visualization work

issues.body:
## Summary
Build the first usable agent inspector drawer/panel on top of the new frontend engine.

## Foundation
This issue assumes the maintainer-owned `frontend/` foundation already exists and that the app can render agent markers plus live snapshot data.

## Scope
- [ ] Build a visible inspector drawer or side panel for a selected agent
- [ ] Show current fields that already exist in the frontend snapshot (name, occupation, location, state, activity, vitals, timestamps)
- [ ] Leave clearly labeled placeholders for future sections like inventory or conversations if needed
- [ ] Keep the component modular so click-to-inspect can wire into it cleanly

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] The inspector can render real selected-agent snapshot data
- [ ] The panel structure is ready for later expansion without over-engineering now
- [ ] The implementation stays focused on UI/state wiring, not new backend architecture

## Notes
If click-to-inspect is not finished yet, it is okay to support selection from a simple temporary list or debug control first.

issues.title: art: create icons for beds, consumables, and common world props

issues.body:
## Summary
Create a first-pass icon set for common interactable world objects and consumables.

## Scope
- [ ] Include beds/sleep-related objects
- [ ] Include food and drink consumables
- [ ] Include common props like chairs, tables, shelves, or signs
- [ ] Organize the export files cleanly and document what is included

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] The icon set is broad enough to be useful across multiple parts of the sim
- [ ] Files are named consistently and easy to browse
- [ ] The contribution is easy for maintainers to review and integrate

## Notes
A compact but well-organized set is better than a huge inconsistent dump.

issues.title: art: create a sprite pack for the six starter NPCs

labels.name: help wanted
labels.description: Extra attention is needed

labels.name: community
labels.description: Open to community contributors and visible on the Townhall board

labels.name: art
labels.description: Visual assets, sprites, tiles, props, and art direction

issues.body:
## Summary
Create a first-pass sprite pack for the six starter Smallville agents.

## Scope
- [ ] Cover Eddy Lin, Isabella Rodriguez, Klaus Mueller, Maria Lopez, Sam Moore, and Abigail Chen
- [ ] Aim for a coherent style across the whole set
- [ ] Include basic facing/idle states if possible, or clearly define what is provided
- [ ] Document dimensions and naming used for exported files

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] All six starter NPCs are represented in a coherent style
- [ ] The assets are organized clearly for future integration
- [ ] Any missing states/limitations are explicitly documented

## Notes
This is a high-visibility contribution and a great way to shape the sim's personality.

issues.title: art: create a starter prop set for cafe, supply store, park, and college scenes

labels.name: help wanted
labels.description: Extra attention is needed

labels.name: community
labels.description: Open to community contributors and visible on the Townhall board

labels.name: art
labels.description: Visual assets, sprites, tiles, props, and art direction

issues.body:
## Summary
Create a starter prop pack that can support several core locations in Smallville.

## Scope
- [ ] Propose and/or create props for Hobbs Cafe, Harvey Oak Supply, Ville Park, and Oak Hill College
- [ ] Focus on reusable scene props (tables, counters, benches, shelves, signs, etc.)
- [ ] Keep assets clearly organized for future frontend/map work
- [ ] Include attribution/license notes if any external references are used

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] The pack covers multiple location types rather than a single room
- [ ] The props are organized and named consistently
- [ ] The contribution is usable as a first-pass asset base for frontend work

## Notes
Partial submissions are okay if they are clearly scoped.

issues.title: art: define a first-pass Smallville visual direction and palette

labels.name: help wanted
labels.description: Extra attention is needed

labels.name: community
labels.description: Open to community contributors and visible on the Townhall board

labels.name: art
labels.description: Visual assets, sprites, tiles, props, and art direction

issues.body:
## Summary
Create a first-pass visual direction for Smallville so art contributors have a shared target.

## Scope
- [ ] Propose a concise visual direction (cozy, readable, slightly stylized, etc.)
- [ ] Suggest a starter palette for town exteriors and key interiors
- [ ] Include references or a small moodboard if helpful
- [ ] Call out any constraints that matter for 2D map readability

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] The proposal is concrete enough for follow-up asset work to reference
- [ ] The direction fits the current project tone and Smallville setting
- [ ] The issue results in a reusable design reference, not just loose ideas

## Notes
This is art direction, not a request to finalize rendering architecture.

issues.title: docs: write a Windows-first contributor quickstart

labels.name: good first issue
labels.description: Good for newcomers

labels.name: community
labels.description: Open to community contributors and visible on the Townhall board

labels.name: docs
labels.description: Documentation, onboarding, and contributor guides

issues.body:
## Summary
Create a contributor quickstart for Windows developers who want to help with `letta-city-sim`.

## Scope
- [ ] Document the minimum prerequisites (Node, Rust, Docker/Postgres, GitHub CLI if relevant)
- [ ] Document how to clone the repo and run the current stack on Windows
- [ ] Document which parts are community-open vs maintainer-owned
- [ ] Document how to find an issue on Townhall and claim it with `/claim`
- [ ] Document how to open a PR back to the repo

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] A new contributor can follow the guide on a Windows machine without prior project context
- [ ] The guide points to canonical docs (`TESTING.md`, PRD, community contributions doc)
- [ ] The guide includes at least one "known gotchas" section for Windows users

## Notes
This is community-open work. Comment `/claim` on this issue if you want to take it on.

issues.title: playtest: compare Claude Sonnet vs Gemini 2.5 Flash for open-world behavior

labels.name: help wanted
labels.description: Extra attention is needed

labels.name: community
labels.description: Open to community contributors and visible on the Townhall board

labels.name: playtest
labels.description: Run simulations, test scenarios, and report findings

issues.body:
## Summary
Compare at least two model backends in similar city-sim scenarios and report differences in behavior, stability, and cost.

## Scope
- [ ] Pick a comparable scenario and run it on at least two models
- [ ] Record notable differences in activity quality, repetition, responsiveness, and cost if known
- [ ] Summarize which model felt better suited for current city-sim needs
- [ ] Share raw notes or logs where practical

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] The comparison is scenario-based rather than purely subjective
- [ ] The report includes trade-offs, not just a winner/loser statement
- [ ] Findings are useful for future maintainer decisions about model defaults

## Notes
This issue is for testing and reporting. It does not change architecture or provider integration.

issues.title: playtest: run a six-agent soak test and capture wake or interrupt anomalies

labels.name: help wanted
labels.description: Extra attention is needed

labels.name: community
labels.description: Open to community contributors and visible on the Townhall board

labels.name: playtest
labels.description: Run simulations, test scenarios, and report findings

issues.body:
## Summary
Run as many of the current starter agents as possible in a longer soak test and document any unstable wake, interrupt, or idle behavior.

## Scope
- [ ] Run a multi-agent session long enough to surface repeated or noisy behavior
- [ ] Watch for repeated wakes, obviously bad loops, or suspiciously silent agents
- [ ] Capture logs and a short written timeline of what happened
- [ ] Summarize the main anomalies in a comment on this issue

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] The report includes agent count, model used, and rough runtime
- [ ] The report identifies specific anomalies with timestamps or log snippets where possible
- [ ] Follow-up issues are created for discrete bugs if necessary

## Notes
This is a great issue for contributors who want to help by running the sim rather than coding.

issues.title: playtest: run a two-agent same-location interaction test and report findings

labels.name: help wanted
labels.description: Extra attention is needed

labels.name: community
labels.description: Open to community contributors and visible on the Townhall board

labels.name: playtest
labels.description: Run simulations, test scenarios, and report findings

issues.body:
## Summary
Run a focused two-agent scenario where both agents share a location and report what happens.

## Scope
- [ ] Pick two starter agents and place them in the same location
- [ ] Run a short scenario that exercises nearby behavior, notices, or manual messages
- [ ] Record whether anything feels awkward, repetitive, or missing
- [ ] Share logs/screenshots/notes in the issue

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] The report includes setup steps and what scenario was tested
- [ ] The report clearly distinguishes expected behavior vs observed behavior
- [ ] Follow-up bugs or UX gaps are called out separately if needed

## Notes
This is especially useful for validating early social/world interaction behavior.

issues.title: playtest: run a 60-minute single-agent unattended session and report loops

labels.name: help wanted
labels.description: Extra attention is needed

labels.name: community
labels.description: Open to community contributors and visible on the Townhall board

labels.name: playtest
labels.description: Run simulations, test scenarios, and report findings

issues.body:
## Summary
Run one agent unattended for roughly 60 minutes and report any loops, stuck behavior, or surprising tool usage.

## Scope
- [ ] Run a single agent with the currently supported world interactions
- [ ] Capture logs, screenshots, or notes when the agent repeats itself or gets stuck
- [ ] Note whether wake/interrupt behavior feels sensible during the run
- [ ] Summarize what happened in a comment on this issue

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] The report includes environment details (model, local setup, rough runtime)
- [ ] The report highlights at least one concrete finding or explicitly says no major issues were observed
- [ ] Any severe bug or loop discovered is linked or split into a follow-up issue

## Notes
This is a playtesting/reporting issue. No code changes are required.

issues.title: docs: write an art and asset contribution guide

labels.name: good first issue
labels.description: Good for newcomers

labels.name: community
labels.description: Open to community contributors and visible on the Townhall board

labels.name: docs
labels.description: Documentation, onboarding, and contributor guides

labels.name: art
labels.description: Visual assets, sprites, tiles, props, and art direction

issues.body:
## Summary
Write a guide for artists and asset contributors who want to help with sprites, props, icons, and map assets.

## Scope
- [ ] Propose a clear folder structure for art submissions based on the current repo layout
- [ ] Document suggested export formats, naming conventions, and dimensions
- [ ] Document what kinds of assets are currently most valuable (tiles, props, NPC sprites, icons)
- [ ] Include a lightweight PR checklist for art contributions

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] A non-engineering contributor can understand how to prepare and submit art assets
- [ ] The guide fits the current project direction (Smallville, frontend planned in Phaser/Next.js)
- [ ] The guide avoids locking in architecture-sensitive rendering decisions

## Notes
This should help non-backend contributors participate quickly.
